claim: IRENA's 2024 World Energy Transitions Outlook estimates clean energy investment in low-income countries needs to increase 7-fold to meet SDG7 and climate goals, with distributed renewables facing the largest financing gaps.
method: Reviewed IRENA report for investment-flow data, financing-gap estimates, technology breakdowns, and country-income-group analysis.
limitations: The 7-fold figure is a modeled projection based on SDG7 and climate scenarios — it depends on access-scenario assumptions and technology-cost projections.
Investment data is aggregated at country level — sub-national financing distribution is not tracked.
Financing-gap estimates combine public and private finance without distinguishing the structural barriers that limit each channel.
The assessment does not capture the 'missing middle' financing structure problem — it quantifies the volume gap but not the access-structure gap.

claim: Concessional climate finance flows disproportionately to utility-scale projects in middle-income countries — the least-developed countries receive a fraction of per-capita clean energy finance relative to their energy-access gaps.

source.title: Climate Policy Initiative — Global Landscape of Climate Finance 2024

method: Reviewed Climate Policy Initiative landscape report for concessional-finance allocation by country income group, technology type, and project scale.
limitations: Finance-flow data is aggregated at country level — sub-national allocation is not systematically tracked.
The disproportionality assessment depends on the comparison metric used (per-capita, per-GDP, per-access-gap) — different metrics give different conclusions.
Concessional finance definitions vary across institutions — comparing across sources requires explicit harmonization.
The assessment does not capture the pipeline of viable projects that cannot access concessional finance — only funded projects are tracked.

claim: National clean energy investment statistics mask enormous sub-national financing gaps — investment concentrates in capital cities and industrial zones while rural and last-mile areas with the largest energy-access gaps receive minimal financing.

source.title: Sub-national clean energy financing gaps: a data assessment

method: Reviewed sub-national financing data availability assessments, investment-geography analysis, and rural-urban financing distribution studies.
limitations: Sub-national financing data is almost entirely absent — the assessment documents the data gap rather than filling it.
The concentration of investment in urban and industrial areas is documented qualitatively but not systematically quantified across countries.
Some countries have project-level data that could enable sub-national analysis, but it is not publicly aggregated.
The assessment does not capture informal financing channels that may serve rural areas outside formal investment tracking.
